intro_band.agent_remit_summary: Devika is declared as an autonomous software-engineering agent that decomposes a user's natural-language project request into a plan, writes code into a per-project workspace, and reports progress back. The remit authorizes a narrow set of capabilities — accepting prompts, planning, code writing, web research for planning, LLM inference via operator-supplied credentials, and a per-project knowledge base — and forbids arbitrary shell execution outside a sandboxed runner, writes outside the project work directory, self-modification, cross-project access, package installs without approval, and credential exfiltration. Every action is required to be recorded to a per-project audit log, untrusted input is required to be screened for injection, and human approval is required for package installs, writes outside touched files, and shell commands whose output was not anticipated in the plan.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python Flask/SocketIO application bound to 0.0.0.0:1337 with no authentication on any route. Entry point devika.py wires an Agent orchestrator in src/agents/agent.py that fans out to twelve sub-agents (planner, researcher, coder, action, runner, feature, patcher, reporter, decision, formatter, internal_monologue, answer). The Runner sub-agent in src/agents/runner/runner.py takes LLM-produced commands and executes them via subprocess.run with no allowlist, no per-command policy, and no approval gate. The advertised sandbox directory src/sandbox/ contains two zero-byte files — code_runner.py and firejail.py — i.e. the sandbox is unimplemented. The settings endpoint POST /api/settings overwrites config.toml (API keys, endpoints, timeouts) without authentication.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is declared sandbox, no sandbox in code, with an LLM-to-shell pipeline already wired up. The Worker Remit forbids arbitrary shell execution outside a sandboxed runner and the architecture document names a sandbox module, but src/sandbox/firejail.py and src/sandbox/code_runner.py are both zero-byte stubs; the actual runtime path in src/agents/runner/runner.py calls subprocess.run directly on commands the LLM produced — no allowlist, no approval gate, no sandbox of any kind. Compounded with an unauthenticated POST /api/settings on a server bound to 0.0.0.0:1337 (anyone on the network can swap the LLM endpoint to attacker-controlled inference) and a path-traversal in Coder.save_code_to_project (LLM-named file paths flow into os.path.join with no commonprefix check), the agent is one prompt away from arbitrary code execution on the operator host — and Devika ships with no audit log, no rate limit, no step-count cap, and no input screening for injection.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Devika may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Devika MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands outside its sandboxed runner.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Devika may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Devika MUST NOT write files outside the configured per-project work directory.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Devika may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Devika MUST NOT install system packages.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Devika may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Devika MUST NOT modify its own code, configuration, or memory store.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Devika may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Devika MUST NOT access other projects' workspaces or memory.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Devika may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Devika MUST NOT exfiltrate operator credentials, API keys, or environment variables.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: All file reads and writes MUST be confined to the current project's work directory, rejecting any path that escapes it.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: The user's natural-language request and any web-retrieved content MUST be treated as untrusted input and screened for instruction injection before it informs LLM context or an action decision.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: Every action — plan step, file write, shell execution, web fetch, LLM call — MUST be recorded to a per-project audit log with enough detail to reconstruct what was done.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: Each run MUST enforce a wall-clock and step-count cap to prevent runaway loops.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: Control MUST return to the user before any action that installs packages, writes outside a previously-touched file, or executes a shell command whose output was not anticipated in the plan.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: The project knowledge base and memory MUST be scoped to a single project, with no content mixed across projects.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must NEVER do
rules.rule_text: Devika MUST NOT read or write files outside the configured work directory by any means — symlink, absolute path, traversal, or shell command.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must NEVER do
rules.rule_text: Devika MUST NOT execute LLM-produced code anywhere but inside the sandboxed runner.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must NEVER do
rules.rule_text: Devika MUST NOT install packages or pull in dependencies without explicit user confirmation, especially from unpinned versions or unverified registries.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Any shell command that would write outside the work directory.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Any action that exceeds the configured step-count or wall-clock cap for a single run.
Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.

findings.summary: LLM-produced shell commands executed via subprocess.run with no sandbox, no allowlist, and no approval gate.
findings.description: The Runner agent receives a list of commands from self.llm.inference(), splits each on whitespace, and passes the result directly to subprocess.run with the project path as cwd. There is no command allowlist, no denylist, no per-command policy, no human approval step, and — given the empty sandbox stubs in finding PRAX-2026-05-29-004 — no isolation layer of any kind. Any prompt that reaches the LLM (user message or web-retrieved content) can name the next shell command.
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands outside its sandboxed runner. / Control MUST return to the user before any action that installs packages, writes outside a previously-touched file, or executes a shell command whose output was not anticipated in the plan. / Devika MUST NOT execute LLM-produced code anywhere but inside the sandboxed runner. / Any shell command that would write outside the work directory.

evidence.snippet: process = subprocess.run(command_set, stdout=PIPE, stderr=PIPE, cwd=project_path) — command_set comes from LLM output via render() then validate_response() at lines 55-67; no allowlist or approval gate
execute() at lines 199-222 — calls self.llm.inference(prompt) then run_code(valid_response, ...) with the returned list of LLM-produced commands

recommended_actions: Add a per-command allowlist enforced before subprocess.run in src/agents/runner/runner.py:85 and :137 and :177; reject anything outside the allowlist with a hard halt and operator alert.
Wire control to the (currently empty) src/sandbox/firejail.py or an equivalent OS-level isolation primitive before any subprocess call, and require an operator approval click for each command list.

findings.summary: Unauthenticated POST /api/settings on 0.0.0.0:1337 overwrites config.toml — API keys, endpoints, and timeouts.
findings.description: The Flask server binds to 0.0.0.0:1337 with no authentication middleware on any route. POST /api/settings takes a JSON body and writes it into config.toml via Config.update_config, including LLM provider endpoints. An attacker on the same network swaps the LLM endpoint to an attacker-controlled host, and every subsequent inference call sends prompts (and receives commands) through the attacker — chaining directly into the Runner RCE in PRAX-2026-05-29-001.
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT modify its own code, configuration, or memory store.

evidence.snippet: @app.route("/api/settings", methods=["POST"]) — calls config.update_config(data) with no auth check; server binds host="0.0.0.0", port=1337 at line 209
update_config() at lines 186-195 — iterates request JSON and writes sub_key/sub_value pairs into config.toml on disk, including API_ENDPOINTS overrides

recommended_actions: Bind the Flask/SocketIO server to 127.0.0.1 by default (devika.py:209) and require a localhost-only or token-authenticated path for /api/settings; reject mutation requests without an operator-presented token.
Restrict /api/settings to a documented allowlist of safe-to-mutate keys and exclude API_ENDPOINTS — endpoint changes should require a config-file edit, not a POST.

findings.summary: Path traversal in Coder.save_code_to_project — LLM-named file paths reach os.path.join with no containment check.
findings.description: save_code_to_project receives a list of {file, code} dicts produced by the LLM, then writes each via open(os.path.join(self.project_dir, project_name, file['file']), "w"). An absolute path or ../ sequence in file['file'] escapes the project directory — os.path.join drops the prefix when the suffix is absolute. Combined with the unauthenticated socket path that triggers a code-writing flow (PRAX-2026-05-29-005), an attacker reaches arbitrary file write on the operator host (~/.ssh/authorized_keys, ~/.bashrc, /etc/cron.d/*, etc).
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT write files outside the configured per-project work directory. / All file reads and writes MUST be confined to the current project's work directory, rejecting any path that escapes it. / Devika MUST NOT read or write files outside the configured work directory by any means — symlink, absolute path, traversal, or shell command.

evidence.snippet: save_code_to_project() at lines 68-80 — file_path = os.path.join(self.project_dir, project_name, file['file']); os.makedirs(file_path_dir); open(file_path, "w") — no os.path.commonprefix or os.path.abspath containment check on file['file']
emulate_code_writing() called from execute() at line 132 with valid_response containing LLM-supplied file dicts; same dicts flow into save_code_to_project via Agent.execute at src/agents/agent.py:357

recommended_actions: In save_code_to_project (coder.py:73-77), after building file_path call os.path.abspath(file_path) and reject any path whose commonpath with the project root is not the project root; raise instead of writing.
Apply the same containment to Feature.save_code_to_project and Patcher.save_code_to_project so all three write paths share one enforcement.

findings.summary: Sandbox module exists in the tree as zero-byte stubs — firejail.py and code_runner.py are unimplemented.
findings.description: The remit forbids executing LLM-produced commands outside a sandboxed runner; the workspace creates a src/sandbox/ directory that names the intended primitives — firejail.py and code_runner.py — but both files are zero bytes. No isolation, no container hardening, no firejail invocation, no chroot, no resource limits. The control was planned and the file slot allocated, then never written.
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands outside its sandboxed runner. / Devika MUST NOT execute LLM-produced code anywhere but inside the sandboxed runner.

evidence.snippet: 0 bytes — empty file, no content whatsoever; the file's name implies firejail-based process isolation that does not exist
0 bytes — empty file; the actual runtime path lives in src/agents/runner/runner.py which calls subprocess.run directly

recommended_actions: Implement src/sandbox/firejail.py to spawn each subprocess inside a firejail (or equivalent OS-level sandbox) profile with no network, read-only system paths, and a writable allowlist scoped to the current project directory.

Implement src/sandbox/code_runner.py as the only call site for subprocess execution, route src/agents/runner/runner.py through it, and add a unit test that fails the build when the runner imports subprocess directly.

findings.summary: Unauthenticated network-reachable agent kickoff — any LAN client can trigger plan/code/run via the user-message socket.
findings.description: The SocketIO server is registered against the same Flask app that binds 0.0.0.0:1337. The user-message event handler reads message, base_model, project_name, and search_engine from the socket payload and spawns a thread that calls Agent.execute or Agent.subsequent_execute — both of which call the LLM, write code, and (via the run action) drop into Runner.run_code. There is no operator identity check, no origin enforcement (the CORS list is hard-coded to localhost:3000 but a socket can ignore that), and no per-message rate limit.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.

evidence.snippet: @socketio.on('user-message') handler at lines 77-103 — spawns Thread(target=lambda: agent.execute(message, project_name)) with no auth or origin gate
socketio.run(app, debug=False, port=1337, host="0.0.0.0") — bound to all interfaces

recommended_actions: Bind to 127.0.0.1 by default in devika.py:209 and require an explicit opt-in env var for LAN exposure; add a SocketIO connect-handshake check that validates a per-session token issued by the local UI.
Rate-limit user-message handling at the SocketIO layer (e.g. flask-limiter) so a runaway client cannot fan out plan/code/run threads in a loop.

findings.summary: Arbitrary file read via GET /api/get-browser-snapshot — send_file takes any caller-supplied path.
findings.description: The route at devika.py:123 accepts a snapshot_path query parameter and passes it directly to Flask's send_file(snapshot_path, as_attachment=True) with no allowlist, no Config.get_screenshots_dir() containment check, and no auth. An attacker on the LAN reads any file the Flask process can open — including config.toml (live API keys), ~/.ssh/*, and the runtime log.
findings.policy_rule_text: All file reads and writes MUST be confined to the current project's work directory, rejecting any path that escapes it. / Devika MUST NOT read or write files outside the configured work directory by any means — symlink, absolute path, traversal, or shell command.

evidence.snippet: browser_snapshot() at lines 123-127 — snapshot_path = request.args.get("snapshot_path"); return send_file(snapshot_path, as_attachment=True); no path containment

recommended_actions: Resolve snapshot_path against Config().get_screenshots_dir(), reject anything whose abspath's commonpath with the screenshots directory is not the screenshots directory, and add auth on the route.

findings.summary: Supply chain is fully unpinned — 33 dependencies floor-free, no lockfile, and devika.dockerfile pipes curl into sudo bash.
findings.description: requirements.txt lists 33 packages with zero version specifiers (no ==, no ~=, no upper bounds) and ships no lockfile — every install pulls the latest published version of flask, openai, anthropic, gevent, playwright, duckduckgo-search, etc. devika.dockerfile:15 additionally fetches and executes the uv installer via curl -fsSL https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sudo -E bash - at build time with no hash verification. A compromised upstream or DNS poisoning yields code execution inside the agent's runtime.
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT install system packages. / Devika MUST NOT install packages or pull in dependencies without explicit user confirmation, especially from unpinned versions or unverified registries.

evidence.snippet: 33 lines (flask, flask-cors, openai, anthropic, gevent, ...) — every package name appears alone with no version specifier; no requirements.lock or pip-compile artifact in the workspace
RUN curl -fsSL https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sudo -E bash - — pipes remote script into sudo bash with no hash check or signature verification

recommended_actions: Pin every line in requirements.txt to a specific version (==X.Y.Z) and add a hash-locked requirements.lock generated via pip-compile or uv pip compile; require CI to fail the build if requirements.txt and the lockfile drift.
Replace the curl|sudo bash install of uv in devika.dockerfile:15 with an apt or pip install pinned to a specific version with a known SHA, or vendor the installer into the repo.

findings.summary: Compound chain — web-fetched content flows unscreened into LLM context that drives subprocess.run-based code execution.
findings.description: Agent.search_queries in src/agents/agent.py fetches the first link's text via browser.extract_text() then passes it through Formatter and into the planner/coder/runner pipeline with no injection screening — the remit's MUST-screen clause is not implemented anywhere in the source. That same pipeline ends at Runner.run_code, which executes LLM-produced commands via subprocess.run with no sandbox (finding PRAX-2026-05-29-004 is empty) and no approval gate. Step-by-step: attacker plants instructions on a search-result page; Devika fetches it; Formatter passes the text through; Coder/Runner translate the injected instructions into commands; subprocess.run executes them on the operator host. This is the canonical indirect-injection-to-RCE chain.
findings.policy_rule_text: The user's natural-language request and any web-retrieved content MUST be treated as untrusted input and screened for instruction injection before it informs LLM context or an action decision. / Devika MUST NOT execute LLM-produced code anywhere but inside the sandboxed runner.

evidence.snippet: search_queries() at lines 79-116 — fetches the first link via web_search.get_first_link(), calls open_page() which returns extract_text(), then passes data straight to formatter.execute() with no provenance label or injection screen

execute() at lines 199-222 — receives the post-planner LLM output as commands and dispatches to run_code() → subprocess.run; the LLM context that produced those commands was seeded by the unscreened web text from agent.py:79

recommended_actions: Implement an explicit injection-screening stage between Browser.extract_text and Formatter — wrap returned content in a labeled untrusted section in the prompt and run a deterministic deny-list / LLM classifier before the content is used; reject pages whose text contains imperative model instructions.

Block the indirect-injection-to-RCE path by gating any Runner.run_code invocation on an explicit operator approval that names the commands, not just by sandboxing — the sandbox is the second layer, the gate is the first.

findings.summary: Required per-project audit log does not exist — runtime logging is one free-form file shared across projects.
findings.description: The remit requires every plan step, file write, shell execution, web fetch, and LLM call to be recorded to a per-project audit log with enough detail to reconstruct what was done. The implementation has one process-wide fastlogging file at data/logs/devika_agent.log that captures Flask route entry/exit and free-form info/debug strings — there is no structured event schema, no per-project segmentation, no record of subprocess commands beyond the in-memory terminal_session state, and no record of LLM prompts unless LOG_PROMPTS is toggled. The log is also exposed unauthenticated via GET /api/logs.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every action — plan step, file write, shell execution, web fetch, LLM call — MUST be recorded to a per-project audit log with enough detail to reconstruct what was done.

evidence.snippet: Logger.__init__ at lines 9-13 — single fastlogging.LogInit on devika_agent.log; no per-project path; route_logger() at lines 40-78 captures Flask request.path / request.method only
real_time_logs() at lines 181-184 — GET /api/logs returns logger.read_log_file() with no auth; entire runtime log served to any caller

recommended_actions: Replace the single shared log with a per-project structured JSONL event log (one record per plan step, code write, subprocess call, web fetch, LLM call) written under data/projects/<project>/audit.jsonl; include timestamps, command lines, file paths, and outcomes.
Add auth on GET /api/logs and scope its output to the requested project's audit log, never the global runtime log.

findings.summary: No step-count or wall-clock cap on agent runs — runaway loop and denial-of-wallet exposure.
findings.description: The remit requires each run to enforce a wall-clock and step-count cap. Neither Agent.execute nor Agent.subsequent_execute in src/agents/agent.py wraps its agent dispatch in a step budget or timeout; Runner.run_code retries failed commands up to twice without bounding the outer plan loop; retry_wrapper in src/services/utils is used throughout. The only timeout in sample.config.toml is the per-inference TIMEOUT.INFERENCE = 60 — that caps a single LLM call, not the agent's total runtime. A malicious or malformed prompt can run the agent until the operator notices, billing inference at every step.
findings.policy_rule_text: Each run MUST enforce a wall-clock and step-count cap to prevent runaway loops. / Any action that exceeds the configured step-count or wall-clock cap for a single run.

evidence.snippet: execute() at lines 270-365 — no step counter, no wall-clock deadline; planner → researcher → search_queries → coder → save_code_to_project all run in a single thread with no outer cap
run_code() at lines 69-197 — retries failed commands while retries < 2 but the outer @retry_wrapper on execute (line 199) can re-enter the loop with no global cap

recommended_actions: Add a top-level step-count and wall-clock budget in Agent.execute and Agent.subsequent_execute (e.g. wrap dispatch in a deadline-checked loop); halt and surface to the operator on exceeded budget.
Make the budgets configurable in sample.config.toml under a new [LIMITS] section and document the defaults; require operator approval to extend.

findings.summary: Operator credentials persisted to plaintext config.toml in the workspace with no vault integration.
findings.description: All nine API provider keys (BING, GOOGLE_SEARCH, CLAUDE, OPENAI, GEMINI, MISTRAL, GROQ, NETLIFY, GOOGLE_SEARCH_ENGINE_ID) are designed to be stored as literal strings in config.toml in the project root. The Config class reads them and provides getter methods used throughout. There is no vault integration, no OS keychain hook, and no env-var fallback path; combined with the arbitrary-file-read in PRAX-2026-05-29-006 and the unauthenticated POST overwrite in PRAX-2026-05-29-002, the configured credentials are simultaneously readable and writable by anyone with LAN access.
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT exfiltrate operator credentials, API keys, or environment variables.

evidence.snippet: [API_KEYS] block at lines 9-18 — nine placeholder API key slots that the running agent populates with literal credentials in config.toml; no vault reference syntax
get_bing_api_key / get_claude_api_key / get_openai_api_key etc. at lines 48-86 — each returns config["API_KEYS"][...] directly, no indirection through a secret manager

recommended_actions: Add an env-var-first credential path (e.g. os.environ.get("DEVIKA_CLAUDE_KEY")) in src/config.py, and document the env-var workflow as the recommended mode; treat config.toml credentials as a deprecated fallback.
Provide an optional adapter for OS keychain (macOS Keychain, Secret Service on Linux) and document it in CONTRIBUTING.md.

findings.summary: No adversarial testing evidence — no test suite, no red-team artifacts, no injection or sandbox-escape scenarios.
findings.description: The workspace contains no tests/ directory under src/, no conftest.py, no documented red-team exercise, no postmortem, and no mention in CONTRIBUTING.md or ARCHITECTURE.md of any adversarial validation. The empty src/sandbox/ stubs are the strongest possible evidence the security model was never exercised — an active red team would have caught the missing sandbox before the runner shipped.

evidence.snippet: No mention of security or adversarial testing in the contribution guide; the document is 2 KB and covers commit conventions only
0-byte sandbox stub — an active red-team process would have caught a security-relevant module landing in main with no implementation

recommended_actions: Add a tests/security/ directory with prompt-injection fixtures (search-result page text that names commands), runner unit tests that assert subprocess.run is never invoked with an LLM-supplied string outside the sandbox path, and path-traversal fixtures for save_code_to_project.
Stand up a documented adversarial review checkpoint in CONTRIBUTING.md — every PR touching agents/, sandbox/, or apis/ must include a red-team note describing the attack scenarios considered.

findings.summary: CORS allowlist covers only Flask routes — SocketIO bypasses it entirely, weakening the apparent same-origin guard.
findings.description: CORS in devika.py:29 is initialized with resources={r"/*": {"origins": [...]}} restricting cross-origin Flask requests to localhost:3000. However, the SocketIO instance at src/socket_instance.py is initialized separately and the user-message handler — which kicks off agent execution — does not inherit that CORS restriction. The control gives a false sense of containment to an operator reading the CORS block.

evidence.snippet: CORS(app, resources={r"/*": {"origins": ["https://localhost:3000", "http://localhost:3000"]}}) — Flask-only, does not configure socketio's allowed origins

recommended_actions: Initialize SocketIO with cors_allowed_origins set to the same list as Flask CORS and document the parity in src/socket_instance.py.

findings.summary: POST /api/run-code is a TODO stub — code parameter is accepted, recorded by route_logger, then silently dropped.
findings.description: The route at devika.py:154 accepts a JSON body with project_name and code, returns a success message, and contains a single TODO: Implement code execution logic. The route is documented neither as deprecated nor as a placeholder; an operator reading the API surface might believe execution is gated through here when the actual execution path is the Runner agent reached via SocketIO. The code parameter is logged in plaintext by the route_logger decorator, which means caller-supplied code snippets accumulate in devika_agent.log with no purpose.

evidence.snippet: run_code() at lines 154-161 — accepts data["code"], TODO comment, returns {"message": "Code execution started"} with no execution attempted; route_logger logs the request body

recommended_actions: Either remove POST /api/run-code from devika.py:154 if the SocketIO path is the only intended kickoff, or implement it as the documented approval-gated entry point and disable the SocketIO path.

findings.summary: Netlify deploy action present in agent dispatch — out-of-remit capability (remit forbids production deployment).
findings.description: Agent.subsequent_execute in src/agents/agent.py:219 handles an action == "deploy" branch that calls Netlify().deploy(project_name) and returns the deploy URL. The remit's Scope Boundaries explicitly state Devika MUST NOT deploy code to production; the Netlify deploy path is a documented production-deploy capability. No approval gate is wired in front of it.

evidence.snippet: deploy branch at lines 219-229 — Netlify().deploy(project_name); deploy_url surfaced back to user as completed action, no approval gate or remit cross-check

recommended_actions: Remove the deploy action branch from src/agents/agent.py:219-229 or gate it behind an explicit operator approval prompt that names the deploy target and credentials in use; document the deploy capability in the remit if it is intended to remain.

rows.purpose: Flask route entry/exit and free-form info/debug strings from agents

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent. Across every RAISE category Devika's controls are either zero-byte stubs (the named sandbox modules), prompt-only (no code-level domain or trust enforcement), or absent entirely (no audit log, no rate limit, no adversarial testing, no dependency pins). The one place credit could be given — secure_filename on project-name parameters in src/apis/project.py — is a partial mitigation that the LLM-controlled file['file'] path in Coder.save_code_to_project bypasses entirely. The agent's posture matches a research-grade demo rather than a production-grade autonomous engineer.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The remit declares a narrow scope (planning, code writing, web research, LLM inference) but there is no code-level enforcement — the system prompt in src/agents/planner through src/agents/runner is the only gate, and the Runner's command list comes straight from the LLM with no domain restriction on what subprocess can run.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Web-fetched content from BingSearch/GoogleSearch/DuckDuckGoSearch in src/agents/agent.py flows through Formatter and into the LLM context with no injection screening despite the remit's MUST-screen clause, and the user's natural-language prompt is concatenated into Jinja2 templates without any trust labeling.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Runner.run_code in src/agents/runner/runner.py passes LLM-produced commands directly to subprocess.run; Coder.save_code_to_project joins LLM-controlled file['file'] into os.path.join with no traversal check; POST /api/settings overwrites config.toml with no authentication on a 0.0.0.0:1337 bind — there are no input validation, no output gating, and no approval gates on any high-impact path.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: requirements.txt lists 33 dependencies with zero version pins (no ==, no lockfile) including flask, openai, anthropic, gevent, playwright, and the devika.dockerfile pulls and runs the uv installer from a remote shell pipe at build time; no SBOM, no provenance documentation, and the sample config carries placeholder API keys for nine providers.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No adversarial test files, no red-team documentation, no postmortems, and no evidence in ARCHITECTURE.md or CONTRIBUTING.md that any injection, RCE, or sandbox-escape scenario was ever exercised against this agent — the empty src/sandbox/ stubs are the strongest possible evidence that the security model was never tested.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: A single rotating devika_agent.log via fastlogging in src/logger.py captures Flask route entry/exit and free-form info/debug strings, but the remit's required per-project audit log of every plan step, file write, shell execution, and LLM call does not exist — and the log file is exposed unauthenticated via GET /api/logs.
